sentences.text: Yes, to Block 6.
Remember I mentioned to you this [NON-ENGLISH], this murderer who was--
No, no.
There was a barber who came from the next town.
He was about 20, 21 years old.
And you know, he used to shave-- they didn't shave me.
I didn't have a beard.
But the head they used to shave, but and they leave a little bit of hair, so much.
In the middle, they used to make like a road, the razor blade completely out, in order they should recognize us that we are prisoners.
That was-- he was the barber.
So what happened?
He did something, or through his friend.
They sold him a little piece of bread, and the bread was stolen from the kitchen.
You know, it was there, in order, you know?
And he knew, they found out, this guy, this murderer, [NON-ENGLISH].
And he knew what is waiting for him.
And he didn't want to wait until he comes to him.
He took the razor and he cut his throat, you know-- in front of me.
And I watched this.
And it was in two seconds, he was dead.
I don't-- I know the town.
They used to call it [NON-ENGLISH]-- Hungarian Eagle, that was the name for the town.
It was maybe, I don't know, 10 kilometers from my town.
I not even remember the name anymore, either.
Yeah, he was the barber for the block.
You know.
That was his job.
Because, apparently a piece of bread was stolen.
So that was the end of him.
So to go back to Block 13.
When before they took us on the death march, something happened there in the barrack.
I didn't know what's going on.
I just saw that less and less people-- the beds are getting empty.
It was full.
There were maybe 700 people sleeping in, you know, five, six, seven in-- you know, they were just skin and bone-- skeleton.
I said, where are they disappearing?
They don't die in the-- and suddenly everyone.
In the night, we had our line, the bed made the wall, who was working for the barrack.
Yeah?
And from me to the right, I though all the sick people who were there in bed, I wanted to know what's going on.
I saw in the back of the barrack something is going on.
You are not supposed to walk around, because you are not supposed to open the light, you know.
Not to give sign for the planes to come to bomb.
I want to know what's going on.
I just go walk between the bed.
And there is a room separated in the back.
And it's covered with, the door is covered with a cover, you know, the blanket.
And what's happening?
I look in, there's a tank of water, yeah, like I don't know, 300, 400 gallon, something like this, from wood around.
And I see there are two or three guys taking old people from the bed, and push the head into the water, and they killing them.
So I get panicky.
I run back to my bed.
And all of a sudden, there were two Ukrainians, also prisoners, but they were well fed, you know.
They were strong, and they were doing the job, killing 700 people in a few nights.
Yeah?
Because I looked out to the window the day, I see dead bodies all the way 10 feet high.
Because the crematoriums, they couldn't burn so many bodies.
So all of a sudden, he comes to me, to my bed, and they want to take me.
So I said, in German, to them, I work, you know, in the other side of the emergency room, with the doctors.
He says he has orders to take me.
So I'm panicky, and I said to the other boys there who are also working, I said, do me a favor.
Run-- it was 2 o'clock in the morning-- run across, you had to get out to the street to go to the other end of the block, you know, because the entrance was from the other side.
Wake up Dr.
Thomas Martinez.
Wake him up and tell him what's happening.
He woke up, he run, and he gave the orders for them.
You don't touch him.
He works here.
So because he gave them the order, and that's the way they left me.
Otherwise, I would be-- so I don't know if it's because I saw what was going on, or he made a mistake.
This, I don't know.
The only thing is, that's the way I found out that there's 700 people were pushed into the water, with the head-- they  kept them two minutes like this, two or three guys.
And when they was dead, they just threw them out of the window.
That was the end of the 700 people.
That was the liquidation before, because they knew, probably, the war is coming to an end.
We didn't know what's going on.
The only thing we know, the bombardment every day, twice a day. 11 o'clock in the morning, 11 o'clock in the evening.
So-- so, I'm sorry, I had to go back, because I didn't want to miss this.
That's OK, that's fine.
Now the American Army came on May 5, 1945.
Yeah.
So what happened when I was laying there already, I was still, you know, I was thinking of course, I was no-- I don't know, I didn't care-- die.
It didn't matter any more.
But I was happy a little bit that, you know, we are liberated.
Everybody left.
I say what's going to be with me?
I going to die here.
Because before the SS run away, the bulldozers dig all those big graves, the mass graves to push everybody in.
That was the plan.
And so anyway the Americans arrive, and I stay there for the night, yeah, in a jeep with American soldiers.
I managed to get into the forest, because the boys who left on the highway-- because the war was still going on-- told them that there is a camp.
And they managed to get in with a jeep.
And they are telling us we have to get out to the road, to the main road, that the Americans should see us, to take us to hospitals, or to-- they made refugee camps right away.
But here, you see, you can all die.
You cannot-- they will not find you.
I says, you know, we spoke a little bit Yiddish, you know?
I says, I cannot go.
I says, I'm too weak.
So he left.
I say, you have to get out there on the road.
So I stay there for the night, you know, laying between the dead.
And next morning, I see it's getting less and less.
Everybody, almost everybody is dead.
Only a few people move a little bit then.
And I'm the only, practically the only one who is still there alive.
So what, I have to get out like they told me, straight down, it was maybe half a mile.
In about the morning, like 7 o'clock, 8 o'clock, I-- you know-- put all the strength in me.
I turn around, and this crawling.
Every, from half a mile, from 7 or 8 o'clock in the morning, I end up 4 o'clock in the afternoon on the road.
I made it.
I made it, and I'm in the ditch there laying.
So the-- I remember the GMC trucks were passing by with the military.
And they saw me there.
And they knew right away, probably, that-- and they stopped and they come to talk to me.
So I don't understand English.
I says, I say Yiddish, or German, or you know, whatever, Czechoslovakian.
I-- I don't speak English.
So all of a sudden, they're bringing over an officer who is a Jew.
He said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]?
I say, yeah.
So he says, come, we're going to put you on the truck, we're going to take you to the hospital, and we're going to help you.
When he mentioned hospital, I don't want to go to the hospital.
I'm afraid they're going to kill me.
In my mind-- I still have the Block 13 in my mind.
He cannot understand why I don't want to go to the hospital.
And I'm weak, I'm dying.
I said, I heard that they are having a refugee camp somewhere in Wels.
Because, you know, the boys who are running around, they come to look, and they tell me Wels, there is a temporary camp there.
If you manage to get there, then they are going to take care of me.
So what's happening?
I said, just put me down in Wels.
I didn't know where Wels is.
So they put me on the truck, and then they help me to go down.
And there's the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, it must be an officers' school or something for the Germans.
And I'm thirsty.
I'm dying.
Just, not hungry.
I couldn't eat.
Just thirsty-- eat, drink.
I see a wagon there.
Someone told me this is the Hungarian Hitler Yugen.
They run away from Hungary when the Russians were coming-- they end up in Austria.
And they-- the horse was not there, just the wagon.
And I climb up on the wagon to look for something, I don't know what, to drink.
Something, I was very, very, I have the fever, you know, I was burning.
So what I see, a big five-gallon can, something like this, from metal-- aluminium, you know?
What's in it?
It's closed.
I have no strength to open it.
And there are little food cans also, with food-- I don't know what it is.
So I take one smaller, and I'm knocking and knocking on this five gallon can.
What's happening?
Finally, I made the hole, I manage to make a hole.
It's filled up with sauerkraut, raw sauerkraut.
Sour, you know?
I take out the kraut, you know, and I take a big dish there.
And there is maybe a couple hundred pounds of sugar in the bag.
I didn't know what I was doing.
I take the sugar, maybe a pound of sugar, and a pound of sauerkraut, raw sauerkraut, and I mix it up together and I eat this.
I'd don't have to tell you when you have diarrhea-- actually I, Dr.
Thomas told me that I have stomach typhus-- the-- [GERMAN] in German.
I don't know in English if they call it the typhoid fever.
That's why I had such a high fever.
And I finished the sauerkraut with the sugar.
What should I tell you?
I collapsed.
I was dead.
This, excuse me, but diarrhea, I made it a thousand percent worse than I was before.
So what shall I do now?
So they climb, they help me, they schlep me into a room.
They're preparing-- they're just getting organized because more prisoners are coming, you know, refugees-- some kind of a bed.
And they put me, just a bed.
Oh, it was fine, you know, the mattress.
I just want to lay, nothing, just lay, nothing, I feel like dying.
So then they, Americans, they give you food.
Yeah?
They came, they start to throw all over the yard, canned food, you know?
Corned beef, stuff like this.
This is poison, but who knew?
You understand?
We open it, eat, this is poison.
People were dying like flies.
Then the Americans realize it's no good to give them so much food.
Then they didn't give us food, just a little bit.
Oh, we start to look at the American-- anti-Semite, they don't give us food.
You know, you are crazy.
You are not normal.
You didn't know what was right.
Then they said there is a doctor-- you know, anybody who is sick, you go to the window and they will give you medication.
I managed to stay in line.
I'm dead tired.
I'm falling down from my feet.
I come to the window.
Who is there?
The doctor.
This Jewish doctor who was in Block 6.
The poor man, he survived.
And he say, Reuben, you're alive.
I say, yeah, but look, I told him what happened, what I did.
He said oh, my God.
This is the worst thing you could do.
This is poison.
He said, listen what I will do with you.
There is thousands of people in line to get medication.
You come in the morning, before 8 o'clock.
You come in the back of the building, and I will open the little window-- because he knew me from Gusen-- and I will give you some opium.
It's a medication.
And this opium, it tasted so good.
It was like sweet, you know?
And I took it, believe it or not, after one or two days, the diarrhea somehow, it wasn't so bad anymore.
I started to gain back a little bit.
I was able to climb up the bed.
I was able to climb down.
They are giving goat milk.
I was able to take a pail and go down.
They were giving out gallons of milk.
And the milk was poison, also.
I didn't know.
So I was drinking the milk.
Again, I fall back to the diarrhea.
And he's telling me, don't eat nothing.
Just a little-- if they give you some crackers or something, they were throwing in this plastic-- I remember the American Army, they had those little crackers.
And I give you the medication.
Come every morning before I open, because they gave him right away the medication, the Americans.
And you're going to be all right.
So a few days goes by and I'm getting, I'm feeling, I'm getting stronger.
I'm able to go already to the washroom.
So the third or forth day, I, you know, I was always to wash myself.
Even in Gusen I used to take showers in winter time.
And they gave us the soap.
I don't know if you know, it was soap made from Jews.
The name was RIF-- Reichs Juden Fett.
I don't know if you ever heard about it.
It was made from Jews, from the Jewish fat-- that kind of soap they gave us.
I didn't know this.
After, I found out.
And I used to take cold showers in the middle of winter just because I couldn't stand to be the-- full of lice.
You know, they are eating us up.
So I am-- I am filthy.
Everybody's robbing the storage spaces, getting clothing, everything.
I still have my old clothing, you know, filthy.
And I'm going to the washroom, try to wash myself and to prepare myself a little bit to see what's going on.
I go into the washroom and all of a sudden, I didn't see myself for a year.
It's a big mirror there, and I look myself, and I faint almost.
It was-- I look skin and bone.
I get scared for myself.
And I am running back to bed.
I say, I'm dying, it's impossible.
So I wash myself and I run back to the bed.
And I tell them, you know, this doctor in the morning, I say, I'm very weak.
He say, don't worry, you just come every morning.
I will give you because I know you.
I want-- you know, he knew me from Block 6.
I will give you the medication.
You're going to be all right.
So that's what happened.
A couple of weeks later already-- we were there four weeks-- so two weeks later I was able to walk, and get out even with some boys to the Australian farmers there around, go to ask some food.
Because the Americans didn't give us enough food.
The reason, because it was no good for us.
So it was not-- I started to get back my appetite.
So the Austrians are very mean people.
Some of them, they send us away, you know, chased us away.
Some of them, they cut a piece of bread and a piece of salami, and they gave it to us.
So all of a sudden I feel like I'm getting my force.
So then everybody in the storage room, they stole all the merchandise there for belong to the Germans.
And I need something, clothing, to throw away these pants and the jacket because I'm full of lice.
The Americans, they put DDT, they spray it in, and that's why I wanted to get, wash myself and feel a little bit clean.
And the only thing I found, it was a German officer uniform, new.
So what should I do?
I'm going to put myself in a German uniform.
I take down all the signs, you know.
It shouldn't look like a German soldier.
And I find a pair of shoes, of boots, you know?
It was-- I needed-- the two feet was still too big to put in, you know?
And I dressed up in the German uniform.
I find the shirt there, and everything.
You know, I look like a German.
So all of a sudden, every day I started to feel better.
I'm stronger, I can walk.
All of a sudden I hear in the loud speaker, anybody who was born in Czechoslovakia should come in and register, or register you want to go maybe to America, they're going to put you in a DP camp.
Or you want to go back to Czechoslovakia, the American Army will take you back to Czechoslovakia.
Of course, only thing I was interested-- to go back to Czechoslovakia to see when I get to Somotor, to see if somebody survived.
So they took us to Pilsen, in military-- the Czechs, we arrived Sunday there.
The Czech people, they brought all their Sunday meal.
They gave their Sunday meal for us, for the refugees.
They didn't have themselves, you know?
And then in Prague, went-- the Joint had already some kind of office-- you know, the Joint.
And they gave us a few crown, something, you know.
At least I had already a few dollars, but it meant maybe three dollars on the black market.
Because if it's five from the crown, a dollar, officially, right away after the war, officially 50 crown a dollar, and 500 crown.
But they gave me about 1,500 crown. 1,500 crown, I can buy a piece of bread on the road, I can do something.
So to go to Somotor, yeah?
To Slovakia.
They telling me you cannot go there because the bridges are destroyed.
You have to go to down river, the [NON-ENGLISH] yeah, to Bratislava.
And then you cannot get through, the train cannot get through.
So from Prague, I managed to go on freight trains, on the top of the trains.
I didn't care.
I just jumped forever.
I ask which direction this train goes.
If it was going closer to Somotor-- I jumped there and I was going.
Ticket, no ticket, freight train, no freight train, passenger train, I didn't ask.
I didn't have to buy, anyway, because I was a refugee.
You know, I had already a little piece of paper that I was liberated in the concentration camp.
So we come to Bratislava, the atmosphere already different.
The Slovaks, they speak Slovak and Hungarian.
They're not friendly, because the Slovaks, you know, they were collaborators-- Tiso was the president-- they were collaborators with the Germans.
Like all other countries also, the Czechs-- they suffer from the Germans.
The Czechs are a different people.
They are fine gentleman.
You know?
So what happened was the closer you get to Kosice around there, you know, to east, they're telling me, you have to go to Budapest, because you cannot get through.
So we have to go to Vienna somehow, Budapest, and I went up on the train that goes to Budapest, you know, at least wherever it goes, closer.
And it was a passenger train.
I don't know where it was.
All of a sudden, the Russians are coming-- document, document, you know, document-- asking papers.
I don't even remember if I had a piece of paper.
I'm not, I'm dressed in the German uniform.
I says, in Slovak, [SLOVAK].
You know [SLOVAK]-- I'm Jewish.
And in the next train, freight train, full of German soldiers.
They're taking them to Siberia.
I say, oh my God, I'm so close home.
And because of my clothing, they're going to take me-- this I knew already, with Siberia, they're taking-- I knew that's the end.
I didn't die.
I managed to survive in the concentration camp, now they're going to take me to Siberia.
And he take me down from the train, and he takes me to his officer.
And he brings over a Jewish officer.
He says, [YIDDISH].
I say, ya.
You understand Yiddish?
I say, I was just liberated now in the [NON-ENGLISH].
I was in the camp, I'm a Jew.
He says, [YIDDISH].
Can you pray?
Can you pray?
And he asked me this.
So I said, [YIDDISH], I tell him the whole thing.
This is the only time God listened to my prayer.
He takes me down, and says don't worry-- I take care of you.
He takes me down with the Russian soldiers there, and they're cooking there.
I couldn't eat still.
The beef, they're boiling it.
He say, here, eat.
He give me vodka, I say I cannot eat.
Just eat, eat.
So that's the way he saved me from going to Siberia.
So I finally arrive in Budapest.
And they're telling me where to go.
There is an organization, the Joint-- you know.
They're going to give you some kind of a paper, and they're going to give you a few Hungarian money.
So I managed to get there because I, all of a sudden I meet somebody on the street.
His name is Klein, who used to be a partisan, you know, in the Russian army, from Somotor.
He survived in the Russian army.
And he says, because Somotor was liberated sometime in October, '44.
I didn't know this, that the Russians were so close already.
We didn't know nothing.
And he says, Hershel, and he's kissing me and hugging me.
I said, do you know by any chance if somebody survived?
And he's telling me, I don't know, he had to think always to never tell the truth.
It was a sickness.
You know, I don't want to mention the name, because in case somebody, you know, in Israel.
He couldn't tell the truth.
He say, your brother, Yankel, the one who died in [NON-ENGLISH] is home.
Your brother David, and I think Zippora.
He thinks.
I was happy.
I have a brother home already.
So I managed to go from Budapest, I leave Budapest.
It must have been some time in June.
I don't know.
Middle of the June.
Because it took me, it was a month I stayed in Wels there.
Three, four weeks to get back on my feet.
And then it took me at least two weeks to manage to get to Somotor.
It was not trains.
It was just a freight train, or a passenger train, it went maybe 50, 100 kilometers.
And then they didn't go further.
It was not the normal circulation yet.
So I was going every time I saw a train.
I knew it goes east.
I jumped up and I was going.
So what did you find in Somotor when you came there?
When I arrived in-- well, I knew already, it was dark, dark, dark.
There I had to change trains, yeah, in [NON-ENGLISH] because there was a train.
It was going-- they called Chop.
Chop was the boarder, the Russian border.
I didn't know that then the Russians took the Carpathians away.
I know Chop in the train has to go through Somotor-- because it's only 11 kilometers from [NON-ENGLISH].
It was in [NON-ENGLISH] already where they get to us, from where they took us.
And I jump out of the train, dark, dark, you could not see nothing.
And I'm going, in Berlin, I have this German bag on my-- army bag.
And then the German uniform.
And I'm going, and somebody told me when you arrived in Somotor, there's a climb.
There is my cousin, Mikael, you know, he's in Israel, he has a factory.
His brother was with me, who died.
I told you that he died, around in March.
He almost made it.
He said that's where every refugee goes there.
You know, and there, they make places there to sleep, and then from there, you know, you get organized and you see what's happening.
So the train stops in Somotor-- dark.
You couldn't see an inch, so dark, because there's no electricity, nothing.
I'm going down the train and I hear talking.
They're talking Yiddish.
See, I go there, who are you?
I say, who are you?
Say, I'm Hershel Reuben.
Hershel Reuben?
I'm [NON-ENGLISH].
Then there's another cousin.
And there's a Spiegel.
About five of us, we arrive the same time, on the same train, without knowing each other, that we are on the same train.
So where you go?
You go to my cousin there.
Everybody goes there, first to spend the evening on the floor, it doesn't matter.
And then in the morning, we going to see what we have to do.
So I'm going down, our house is the first house from the railroad station, because that's where we had the tavern.
So I went down the street and I look the house.
The weeds up to the roof.
Windows broken out.
This is terrible, look at this.
That's where I was born.
There's nothing there.
I just make a right turn, you know, and I'm going to my cousin.
We come there, we open the door, so everybody's happy.
Hershel, you know, where were you, where were you?
And I tell them the story.
His name is Meckel, the same like I had a brother Meckel who died of appendix infection.
It was-- it was too late for him.
And I tell him, Meckel, your brother Mordecai, he was with me, and I helped him.
I had it not bad on the end, and I fed him.
In March, end of March sometime, he get the diarrhea, he get sick, and we couldn't, he couldn't make it.
He died.
So that was then.
So we fell asleep for an hour or two because we are dead tired.
So where are am I going?
My mother had a friend, a Gentile Christian lady.
Should I stop?
OK, this is the end of the fourth tape.
